unicode.value: Unit 4: Problem solving at work - Session 1
Juan Ignacio Plaza Rodríguez

Todas las imágenes incluidas en esta unidad han sido generadas mediante inteligencia artificial a través de la herramienta ChatGPT (OpenAI). Estas imágenes han sido creadas exclusivamente para uso educativo y no reproducen contenido preexistente. No están sujetas a derechos de autor de terceros y pueden ser utilizadas dentro del marco del proyecto educativo sin fines comerciales. Se aplican los términos de uso y licencias correspondientes al servicio de generación de imágenes de OpenAI, que permiten su uso para fines educativos y de desarrollo de materiales propios.
master class
closed exercise or problem
real project
didactic game
open problem
conceptual map
Sección
Unit 4: Problem solving at work - Session 1

Session 1 – Workplace Problems and Solutions

Introduction

Starting a new project, solving an issue, or reporting a mistake are all part of daily work life. In this session, you’ll learn how to discuss common workplace problems and how professionals address them.

Learning Objectives

Through reading, vocabulary, and grammar activities, you’ll learn to:

  • Understand and describe typical workplace challenges.
  • Identify causes and consequences of problems.
  • Use appropriate words to describe issues and solutions.
  • Practice conditional sentences to discuss consequences and make suggestions.

Skills You'll Develop

You’ll practice reading skills and review useful verbs and expressions to explain, report, and solve real-world professional situations.

  • Reading comprehension of workplace scenarios.
  • Using precise vocabulary to articulate problems and solutions.
  • Applying conditional sentences in professional communication.

Why This Matters

This session will equip you to handle problems and communicate solutions with clarity and confidence.

Let’s get started and tackle workplace challenges like a pro!
Text and Tasks
1. Reading

Reading: Workplace Challenges 📘

Introduction

Have you ever had a problem at work or during an internship? Maybe a colleague didn’t answer your message, or you had too many tasks and not enough time. Problems like these happen in every job — the important thing is knowing how to handle them.

In this reading activity, you’ll read about some of the most common challenges people face at work and discover useful ways to deal with them. You’ll see how clear communication, cooperation, and flexibility can help solve or even prevent many difficulties.

Reading tip: While you read, try to identify the problem in each paragraph and then look for the solution or advice. This will help you understand the structure of the text and remember key ideas more easily.

Also, pay attention to the cause-and-effect relationships — they are often expressed with conditional sentences, which you’ll practise in the grammar section later.

Ready? Let’s explore how to turn problems into learning opportunities.
Text and Tasks

Common Problems at Work – and How to Deal with Them 📖

Reading Text 🌟

Most people face challenges at work during their careers. These problems can be small, like a misunderstanding with a colleague, or more serious, like constant stress or a lack of support. If problems are not managed in time, they can affect performance, health and motivation. Fortunately, many situations can be improved with clear communication, planning and a positive attitude.

One of the most common issues is poor communication. If people don’t explain tasks clearly or listen to each other, mistakes happen. This can lead to delays, confusion, or tension in the team. If colleagues listened more carefully and asked questions when they weren’t sure, many problems could be avoided. It is also useful to give and receive feedback in a respectful way.

Adapting to change is another frequent challenge. Workplaces often introduce new tools, new procedures or new team members. If employees are flexible and open to learning, they will find change easier to manage. Resistance usually increases stress and reduces efficiency. Viewing change as an opportunity to grow can make a big difference.

Heavy workload is also a major cause of stress. When the volume of work is too high, it can affect productivity and job satisfaction. If tasks are prioritised and time is organised effectively, workers feel more in control. It also helps to talk to supervisors and ask for support when necessary. If teams cooperated more and shared responsibilities, the pressure would decrease.

Conflicts between coworkers can happen in any company. Different personalities, communication styles or expectations can cause tension. If these problems are ignored, they usually get worse over time. Talking calmly, listening actively and trying to understand the other person’s point of view are key strategies. Respectful communication helps maintain a positive environment.

Work-life balance is another important aspect of a healthy job. Long working hours and constant pressure can have negative effects on both physical and mental health. If employees don’t rest enough, they can’t perform well. Taking regular breaks, disconnecting outside work hours and asking for flexible conditions can reduce burnout and improve motivation.

In summary, workplace problems are common, but they don’t have to be permanent. If people communicate clearly, stay flexible and ask for help when needed, they will be more prepared to solve challenges and enjoy their work more.

Adapted from: SEEK: Common workplace challenges and how to overcome them

Unit 4: Changes at Work – Session 1: Reading Practice
Text and Tasks

Reading Comprehension Activities 📖

Activity 1: True or False 🌟

Read the statements below. Are they true or false according to the text?

💡 Read each question carefully and refer to the text to choose the correct answer.

  • 1. If you ignore a conflict with a colleague, it usually disappears.

  • 2. Organising tasks and setting priorities can help reduce stress.

  • 3. Listening carefully and asking questions can improve communication.

  • 4. The text suggests avoiding help when the workload is too heavy.

Activity 2: Multiple Choice 🌟

Choose the best option for each question based on the text.

💡 Read each question carefully and refer to the text to choose the correct answer.

  • 5. What happens if employees don’t get enough rest?

  • 6. What helps workers deal with change more effectively?

  • 7. How does the text recommend resolving conflicts?

Activity 3: Match the Cause and the Effect 🌟

Match the problems with their possible consequences or solutions according to the text.

💡 Read each question carefully and refer to the text to choose the correct answer.

  • 8. A. Heavy workload

  • 9. B. Changes at work

  • 10. C. Poor communication

  • 11. D. Lack of rest

Activity 4: Word from Definition 🌟

Choose the correct word from the text based on the definition provided.

💡 Read each question carefully and refer to the text to choose the correct answer.

  • 12. The ability to accept change and adapt to new situations.

  • 13. A negative feeling that appears when there is too much pressure or work.

  • 14. A situation where someone doesn’t understand something correctly.

  • 15. What people should do when there is a problem between colleagues.

Click “Check Answers” to see your score.

Unit 4: Changes at Work – Session 1: Reading Comprehension
Text and Tasks
2. Use of English

Use of English: Conditional Sentences 🧠

How to Talk About Consequences and Possibilities

In the workplace, actions often have consequences. If someone misses a deadline, the team feels the pressure. If colleagues communicate well, everything works more smoothly. These are examples of conditional sentences.

We use conditionals to:

  • State general rules or routines (Zero Conditional)
  • Talk about possible future situations (First Conditional)
  • Imagine alternative scenarios or give advice (Second Conditional)

This section will help you learn how to use all three forms to describe real workplace problems and propose effective solutions — just like in the reading activity.

Let’s discover how conditionals can make your English clearer, more professional, and more useful at work.
Text and Tasks
2.1. Diagnostic test

Get Ready to Check Your Grammar! 🧠

Before we explore how to use conditional sentences at work, let’s take a moment to see what you already know. This diagnostic test will help you review the three most common conditional forms in English — and understand when and why we use them:

  • Zero Conditional – for general truths and workplace rules
  • First Conditional – for real and possible future situations
  • Second Conditional – for imaginary or hypothetical ideas and polite advice

What’s the goal? 🏁

To help you identify which structures you already use well — and which ones you might want to review before moving on.

How? ✅

Take the test and check your answers at the end using the Answer Key.

Scoring: 📊

  • 80% or more (16–20 points): Great! You’re ready for the next step.
  • Less than 80% (below 16 points): No worries — review the grammar section and try again with confidence.

This activity supports your Competence to Learn to Learn and your Competence for Lifelong Learning — both essential for success in today’s workplace.

Unit 4: Changes at Work – Session 1: Diagnostic Test Introduction
Text and Tasks

Diagnostic Test – Conditionals 🧪

Part 1: Multiple Choice 🔹

Choose the best answer to complete the sentence.

💡 Use Zero Conditional for general facts, First Conditional for real future possibilities, and Second Conditional for hypothetical situations.

  • 1. If you don’t water the plant, it __________.

  • 2. If I were the manager, I __________ more training.

  • 3. If she works overtime, she __________ more tired.

  • 4. If you arrive late again, the supervisor __________.

  • 5. If the lights go out, the emergency system __________ on.

  • 6. If the team shared responsibilities, workers __________ less stressed.

  • 7. If they don’t take breaks, their productivity __________.

  • 8. If I had more time, I __________ a second internship.

Part 2: Complete the Sentence 🔹

Write the correct verb form in the blank.

💡 Use Zero Conditional for general facts, First Conditional for real future possibilities, and Second Conditional for hypothetical situations.

  • 9. If you __________ (not follow) instructions, you make mistakes.

  • 10. If we finish early, we __________ (help) the logistics team.

  • 11. If he __________ (be) more organised, he would meet deadlines.

  • 12. If employees __________ (feel) safe, they work better.

  • 13. If the machine broke, we __________ (call) technical support.

Part 3: Identify the Conditional Type 🔹

Choose the correct type of conditional used in each sentence.

💡 Use Zero Conditional for general facts, First Conditional for real future possibilities, and Second Conditional for hypothetical situations.

  • 14. If you train regularly, you improve your performance.

  • 15. If we reduce the number of emails, we’ll have more time to focus.

  • 16. If she were more confident, she would speak in meetings.

  • 17. If workers cooperate, productivity increases.

Part 4: Error Detection 🔹

Choose the sentence that is grammatically correct.

💡 Use Zero Conditional for general facts, First Conditional for real future possibilities, and Second Conditional for hypothetical situations.

  • 18.

  • 19.

  • 20.

Click “Check Answers” to see your score.

Unit 4: Changes at Work – Session 1: Diagnostic Test
Text and Tasks
2.2. Grammar Lesson

Grammar Focus: Conditional Sentences 🧠

Why Conditionals Matter in the Workplace

Conditional sentences help you explain cause-and-effect, give advice, or imagine solutions to workplace challenges, like those in the reading Common Problems at Work. They structure how we think about real problems (e.g., poor communication) and possible fixes (e.g., listening actively). For example:

  • If you don’t communicate clearly, mistakes happen.
  • If I had better work-life balance, I’d feel more motivated.

Let’s explore three conditional types, using examples from the reading to connect grammar to workplace communication.

🎧 Complementary Audio – Guided Explanation
This audio supports you as you go through the lesson. Listen to it for a step-by-step explanation with spoken examples. It’s especially helpful if you prefer learning by listening or want to review while doing other tasks.

Zero Conditional: Workplace Truths

Use the zero conditional for general truths or routines that always occur under specific workplace conditions.

Structure: If + present simple, present simple

Examples (from reading):

  • If employees don’t communicate clearly, mistakes happen.
  • If workers resist change, stress increases.
  • If teams cooperate, pressure decreases.

Purpose: Describe consistent workplace rules or consequences.

Note: Use “when” instead of “if” for routines (e.g., When conflicts arise, communication helps).

First Conditional: Realistic Solutions

Use the first conditional for likely outcomes or practical solutions to workplace issues in the present or future.

Structure: If + present simple, will + base verb

Examples (from reading):

  • If you organize your tasks, you will reduce stress.
  • If colleagues listen actively, conflicts will decrease.
  • If you take regular breaks, you will improve motivation.

Purpose: Offer advice, predict results, or plan actions.

Tip: Use “can,” “may,” or “should” for variety (e.g., If you ask for help, you can manage your workload).

Second Conditional: Hypothetical Improvements

Use the second conditional for imaginary or unlikely scenarios, ideal for suggesting workplace improvements or giving polite advice.

Structure: If + past simple, would + base verb

Examples (from reading):

  • If we had better work-life balance, we would feel more motivated.
  • If I were the manager, I would prioritize communication.
  • If teams shared responsibilities, workers would feel less stressed.

Purpose: Suggest changes or reflect on alternatives tactfully.

Note: Use “were” for all subjects (e.g., If I were you, I’d talk to the supervisor).

Comparing Conditionals

Each conditional addresses workplace challenges differently:

Type Function Example Real or Imaginary?
Zero Rules / Facts If employees don’t communicate, mistakes happen. Always true
First Likely solutions If you organize tasks, you’ll reduce stress. Real / possible
Second Hypothetical fixes If we had better balance, we’d be motivated. Imaginary / unlikely

Practice: Communicate with Conditionals

Use these prompts, inspired by the reading, to create conditional sentences. Speak with a partner or write your answers:

  • Workplace Rule: Describe a communication rule.
    Example: If team members don’t listen, confusion happens.
  • Advice for Workload: Suggest a way to manage stress.
    Example: If you prioritize tasks, you’ll feel more in control.
  • Ideal Workplace: Imagine a change to reduce conflicts.
    Example: If we had open discussions, teams would collaborate better.

Discuss with a partner: How do conditionals help you solve workplace problems like those in the reading?

Master conditionals to tackle workplace challenges with clear, professional communication!
Text and Tasks
2.3. Practice

Grammar Practice – Conditionals 🧠

Activity 1: Understanding the Speaker’s Perspective 🌟

Read each sentence and choose how the speaker sees the situation.

💡 Use Zero Conditional for general facts, First Conditional for real future possibilities, and Second Conditional for hypothetical situations.

  • 1. If employees feel supported, they do better work.

  • 2. If we finish early, we’ll help the marketing team.

  • 3. If I had more free time, I would do a professional course.

Activity 2: Same Situation, Different View 🌟

Match each version of the situation with the mental frame it expresses.

💡 Use Zero Conditional for general facts, First Conditional for real future possibilities, and Second Conditional for hypothetical situations.

  • 4. A. If you arrive late, the team starts without you.

  • 5. B. If you arrive late, the manager will talk to you.

  • 6. C. If you arrived late every day, people would stop trusting you.

Activity 3: Fill the Gap: Understanding Use 🌟

Complete each sentence with the correct form of the verb. Think: is it a rule, a plan, or an imaginary scenario?

💡 Use Zero Conditional for general facts, First Conditional for real future possibilities, and Second Conditional for hypothetical situations.

  • 7. If people ___ clearly, mistakes happen.

  • 8. If the company shares more information, people ___ safer.

  • 9. If employees had more control, they ___ more motivated.

Activity 4: What Kind of “if” is it? 🌟

Read each sentence. What does “if” express? Choose all that apply.

💡 Use Zero Conditional for general facts, First Conditional for real future possibilities, and Second Conditional for hypothetical situations.

  • 10. If teams cooperate, stress goes down.

  • 11. If I worked in that department, I’d try a different method.

  • 12. If we reduce overtime, workers will be more focused.

Complete all activities to see your total score.

Unit 4: Changes at Work – Session 1: Grammar Practice
Text and Tasks
3. Vocabulary

Vocabulary and Practice: Workplace Problems and Solutions 🛠️

Words matter — especially at work. When something goes wrong, it’s important to describe the situation clearly and professionally. Is it a delay? A mistake? A technical issue? And what happens next — do you investigate, report, replace, or compensate?

In this section, you’ll practise using vocabulary to describe real workplace situations and how to respond to them. You’ll see how different problems can affect your team, clients, or deadlines — and what actions professionals take to solve them.

You’ll work with two key vocabulary areas:

  • Problems and Causes – to describe what’s going wrong
  • Solutions and Actions – to express how to deal with those problems

Use these words in the exercises to prepare for writing reports, making suggestions, and communicating more effectively at work. Ready? Let’s get started.

Unit 4: Changes at Work – Session 1: Vocabulary and Practice Introduction
Text and Tasks
3.1. Vocabulary Focus

Vocabulary Focus: Workplace Problems and Solutions 🛠️

Why This Vocabulary Matters

No job is perfect — and from time to time, things go wrong. A delay, a mistake, a lack of support or miscommunication can create tension, reduce efficiency, or even affect people’s well-being. But knowing how to describe these problems — and how to talk about possible solutions — helps you become a more effective and professional communicator.

In this session, you’ll learn vocabulary to:

  • Identify common workplace problems and their causes
  • Talk about the effects of problems on teams, clients, or deadlines
  • Describe how companies respond to mistakes or complaints
  • Use clear and professional verbs to suggest solutions

Let’s dive into two key areas: Workplace Problems and Causes and Solutions and Actions.
🎧 Complementary Audio – Guided Explanation
This audio supports you as you go through the lesson. Listen to it for a step-by-step explanation with spoken examples. It’s especially helpful if you prefer learning by listening or want to review while doing other tasks.

1. Workplace Problems and Causes

How do you describe a situation where something goes wrong? These nouns are essential when you want to explain a problem or identify its origin. You’ll use them in reports, emails, or when talking to your manager or colleagues.

Noun Meaning Example
malfunction A machine, system, or device that stops working correctly There was a malfunction in the scanner during the audit.
delay When something happens later than expected The shipment arrived with a 3-day delay.
poor communication When people don’t share or understand information properly The conflict started because of poor communication.
miscommunication Incorrect or unclear information between people A miscommunication caused the wrong file to be sent.
overload Too much work or pressure Many workers are experiencing overload this week.
stress Mental or emotional pressure caused by work Constant stress affects productivity and morale.

Tip: Use “due to” or “because of” to explain causes. Example: The meeting was delayed due to a technical malfunction.

2. Solutions and Actions

Now that you can describe the problems, how do you talk about solving them? These verbs are common in professional communication — from formal reports to team conversations.

Verb Meaning Example
investigate To examine the cause of a problem The manager will investigate the complaint before responding.
resolve To find a solution to a problem We resolved the issue by replacing the broken component.
prevent To stop something from happening Proper training can prevent accidents in the workplace.
report To officially inform someone about a problem Please report any incidents to your supervisor immediately.
replace To change something that is broken or not working The IT team replaced the server after the failure.
compensate To offer something (money, time, service) to fix a problem The company compensated the client for the delivery error.

Tip: Use verbs like investigate, resolve, and compensate in formal writing. Report and replace are common in emails or when giving instructions.

How to Use This Vocabulary

This vocabulary will help you participate in conversations about real work challenges. Try using these words and expressions in:

  • Emails: We’re working to resolve the issue as soon as possible.
  • Meetings: Let’s investigate the cause of the delay.
  • Reports: The malfunction was reported and the part was replaced.
  • Instructions: Report the problem immediately and stop the machine.

You’ll also use this vocabulary in the unit tasks, especially when describing workplace situations or giving advice in writing.

Unit 4: Changes at Work – Session 1: Vocabulary Focus
Text and Tasks
3.2. Practice
Provide instruction on how the cloze activity should be completed. Default text will be entered if there are no changes to this field.
University of Auckland
If this option is checked, submitted answers with different capitalization will be marked as incorrect.

Vocabulary Exercises: Workplace Problems and Solutions 🛠️

Exercise 1 – Listen and complete the missing words

You will hear an employee explain a problem they experienced at work. Listen carefully and complete the sentences with the missing words from the audio. All the missing words are common in workplace communication.

“This morning we had to stop production because of a serious technical malfunction in one of the machines. The system suddenly stopped working during the first shift. We immediately informed the team leader, who decided to report the issue to maintenance. While they tried to investigate the cause, we had to delay two deliveries. Fortunately, they managed to resolve the problem after about 45 minutes. To avoid this in the future, the supervisor said we need to review our protocols and do more checks to prevent similar issues. If the problem had continued, we might have had to compensate the affected clients.”
If this option is set, each word will be marked as the learner types it rather than all the words being marked the end of the exercise.

Enter the text for the cloze activity in to the cloze field by either pasting text from another source or by typing text directly into the field.

To select words to hide, double click on the word to select it and click on the Hide/Show Word button below.

More than one possible answer can be defined enclosing them with pipes (|). I.e.: |dog|cat|bird|

If left unchecked a small number of spelling and capitalization errors will be accepted. If checked only an exact match in spelling and capitalization will be accepted.

For example: If the correct answer is Elephant then both elephant and Eliphant will be judged "close enough" by the algorithm as it only has one letter wrong, even if "Check Capitalization" is on.

If capitalization checking is off in the above example, the lowercase e will not be considered a mistake and eliphant will also be accepted.

If both "Strict Marking" and "Check Capitalization" are set, the only correct answer is "Elephant". If only "Strict Marking" is checked and "Check Capitalization" is not, "elephant" will also be accepted.

Vocabulary Exercises: Workplace Problems and Solutions 🛠️

Exercise 1 – Listen and complete the missing words

You will hear an employee explain a problem they experienced at work. Listen carefully and complete the sentences with the missing words from the audio. All the missing words are common in workplace communication.

“This morning we had to stop production because of a serious technical malfunction in one of the machines. The system suddenly stopped working during the first shift. We immediately informed the team leader, who decided to report the issue to maintenance. While they tried to investigate the cause, we had to delay two deliveries. Fortunately, they managed to resolve the problem after about 45 minutes. To avoid this in the future, the supervisor said we need to review our protocols and do more checks to prevent similar issues. If the problem had continued, we might have had to compensate the affected clients.”

Vocabulary Exercises: Workplace Problems and Solutions 🛠️

Exercise 1 – Listen and complete the missing words

You will hear an employee explain a problem they experienced at work. Listen carefully and complete the sentences with the missing words from the audio. All the missing words are common in workplace communication.

“This morning we had to stop production because of a serious technical
in one of the machines. The system suddenly stopped working during the first shift. We immediately informed the team leader, who decided to
the issue to maintenance. While they tried to
the cause, we had to delay two deliveries. Fortunately, they managed to
the problem after about 45 minutes. To avoid this in the future, the supervisor said we need to review our protocols and do more checks to
similar issues. If the problem had continued, we might have had to
the affected clients.”

Cloze exercises are texts or sentences where students must fill in missing words. They are often used for the following purposes:

  1. To check knowledge of core course concepts (this could be a pre-check, formative exercise, or summative check).
  2. To check reading comprehension.
  3. To check vocabulary knowledge.
  4. To check word formation and/or grammatical competence.

If your goal is to test understanding of core concepts or reading comprehension

Write a summary of the concept or reading long enough to adequately test the target's knowledge, but short enough not to induce fatigue. Less than one typed page is probably adequate, but probably considerably less for young students or beginners.

Select words in the text thatare key to understanding the concepts. Thesewill probably be verbs, nouns, and key adverbs.Choose alternatives with one clear answer.

If your goal is to test vocabulary knowledge

Write a text using the target vocabulary. This text should be coherent and cohesive, and be of an appropriate length. Highlight the target words in the text. Choose alternatives with one clear answer.

If your goal is to test word formation/grammar:

Write a text using the target forms. This text should be coherent and cohesive, and be of an appropriate length. Remember that the goal is not vocabulary knowledge, so the core meanings of the stem words should be well known to the students.

Highlight the target words in the text. Provide alternatives with the same word stem, but different affixes. It is a good idea to get a colleague to test the test/exercise to make sure there are no surprises!
Cloze Activity
Enter any feedback you wish to provide the learner with-in the feedback field. This field can be left blank.
Provide instruction on how the cloze activity should be completed. Default text will be entered if there are no changes to this field.
University of Auckland
If this option is checked, submitted answers with different capitalization will be marked as incorrect.

Exercise 2 – Listen and complete the missing words📝

You will hear an employee describe a difficult week at work. Listen carefully and fill in the blanks with the missing words. All of them are related to workplace problems and their causes.

“This week has been challenging for our team. First, we had a long delay with the delivery of materials, which pushed everything back by almost two days. Then, there was some serious miscommunication between our department and logistics. We expected the goods on Monday, but they thought the deadline was Wednesday.

To make things worse, two people were off sick, so the rest of the team experienced serious overload . Everyone had too many tasks and not enough time. On Thursday, the stress levels were so high that one of our colleagues made a critical error in the shipment list.”
If this option is set, each word will be marked as the learner types it rather than all the words being marked the end of the exercise.

Enter the text for the cloze activity in to the cloze field by either pasting text from another source or by typing text directly into the field.

To select words to hide, double click on the word to select it and click on the Hide/Show Word button below.

More than one possible answer can be defined enclosing them with pipes (|). I.e.: |dog|cat|bird|

If left unchecked a small number of spelling and capitalization errors will be accepted. If checked only an exact match in spelling and capitalization will be accepted.

For example: If the correct answer is Elephant then both elephant and Eliphant will be judged "close enough" by the algorithm as it only has one letter wrong, even if "Check Capitalization" is on.

If capitalization checking is off in the above example, the lowercase e will not be considered a mistake and eliphant will also be accepted.

If both "Strict Marking" and "Check Capitalization" are set, the only correct answer is "Elephant". If only "Strict Marking" is checked and "Check Capitalization" is not, "elephant" will also be accepted.

Exercise 2 – Listen and complete the missing words📝

You will hear an employee describe a difficult week at work. Listen carefully and fill in the blanks with the missing words. All of them are related to workplace problems and their causes.

“This week has been challenging for our team. First, we had a long delay with the delivery of materials, which pushed everything back by almost two days. Then, there was some serious miscommunication between our department and logistics. We expected the goods on Monday, but they thought the deadline was Wednesday.

To make things worse, two people were off sick, so the rest of the team experienced serious overload . Everyone had too many tasks and not enough time. On Thursday, the stress levels were so high that one of our colleagues made a critical error in the shipment list.”

Exercise 2 – Listen and complete the missing words📝

You will hear an employee describe a difficult week at work. Listen carefully and fill in the blanks with the missing words. All of them are related to workplace problems and their causes.

“This week has been challenging for our team. First, we had a long
with the delivery of materials, which
everything back by almost two days. Then, there was some serious

between our department and logistics. We expected the goods on Monday, but they thought the deadline was Wednesday.

To make things worse, two people were off sick, so the rest of the team experienced serious
. Everyone had too many tasks and not enough time. On Thursday, the
levels were so high that one of our colleagues made a critical
in the shipment list.”

Cloze exercises are texts or sentences where students must fill in missing words. They are often used for the following purposes:

  1. To check knowledge of core course concepts (this could be a pre-check, formative exercise, or summative check).
  2. To check reading comprehension.
  3. To check vocabulary knowledge.
  4. To check word formation and/or grammatical competence.

If your goal is to test understanding of core concepts or reading comprehension

Write a summary of the concept or reading long enough to adequately test the target's knowledge, but short enough not to induce fatigue. Less than one typed page is probably adequate, but probably considerably less for young students or beginners.

Select words in the text thatare key to understanding the concepts. Thesewill probably be verbs, nouns, and key adverbs.Choose alternatives with one clear answer.

If your goal is to test vocabulary knowledge

Write a text using the target vocabulary. This text should be coherent and cohesive, and be of an appropriate length. Highlight the target words in the text. Choose alternatives with one clear answer.

If your goal is to test word formation/grammar:

Write a text using the target forms. This text should be coherent and cohesive, and be of an appropriate length. Remember that the goal is not vocabulary knowledge, so the core meanings of the stem words should be well known to the students.

Highlight the target words in the text. Provide alternatives with the same word stem, but different affixes. It is a good idea to get a colleague to test the test/exercise to make sure there are no surprises!
Cloze Activity
Enter any feedback you wish to provide the learner with-in the feedback field. This field can be left blank.
Provide instruction on how the cloze activity should be completed. Default text will be entered if there are no changes to this field.
University of Auckland
If this option is checked, submitted answers with different capitalization will be marked as incorrect.

Exercise 3 – Complete the sentences with the correct word ✏️

Instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct word from the list.

Words: malfunction – delay – report – investigate – resolve – stress – overload – miscommunication – error – compensate

  1. We had to stop the production line due to a technical malfunction .
  2. A delivery delay  caused problems with our client’s schedule.
  3. Please report  any equipment issues to your supervisor immediately.
  4. The IT team needs more time to investigate  the cause of the system crash.
  5. It took us two hours to resolve  the issue with the payment system.
  6. Constant deadlines and pressure can lead to high levels of stress .
  7. With three people off work, the rest of the team experienced serious overload .
  8. A small miscommunication  between departments caused a major billing mistake.
  9. The mistake in the invoice was a human error , but it affected the whole process.
  10. The company decided to compensate  the customer with a discount on their next order.
If this option is set, each word will be marked as the learner types it rather than all the words being marked the end of the exercise.

Enter the text for the cloze activity in to the cloze field by either pasting text from another source or by typing text directly into the field.

To select words to hide, double click on the word to select it and click on the Hide/Show Word button below.

More than one possible answer can be defined enclosing them with pipes (|). I.e.: |dog|cat|bird|

If left unchecked a small number of spelling and capitalization errors will be accepted. If checked only an exact match in spelling and capitalization will be accepted.

For example: If the correct answer is Elephant then both elephant and Eliphant will be judged "close enough" by the algorithm as it only has one letter wrong, even if "Check Capitalization" is on.

If capitalization checking is off in the above example, the lowercase e will not be considered a mistake and eliphant will also be accepted.

If both "Strict Marking" and "Check Capitalization" are set, the only correct answer is "Elephant". If only "Strict Marking" is checked and "Check Capitalization" is not, "elephant" will also be accepted.

Exercise 3 – Complete the sentences with the correct word ✏️

Instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct word from the list.

Words: malfunction – delay – report – investigate – resolve – stress – overload – miscommunication – error – compensate

  1. We had to stop the production line due to a technical malfunction.
  2. A delivery delay caused problems with our client’s schedule.
  3. Please report any equipment issues to your supervisor immediately.
  4. The IT team needs more time to investigate the cause of the system crash.
  5. It took us two hours to resolve the issue with the payment system.
  6. Constant deadlines and pressure can lead to high levels of stress.
  7. With three people off work, the rest of the team experienced serious overload.
  8. A small miscommunication between departments caused a major billing mistake.
  9. The mistake in the invoice was a human error, but it affected the whole process.
  10. The company decided to compensate the customer with a discount on their next order.

Exercise 3 – Complete the sentences with the correct word ✏️

Instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct word from the list.

Words: malfunction – delay – report – investigate – resolve – stress – overload – miscommunication – error – compensate

  1. We had to stop the production line due to a technical

.
  • A delivery

caused problems with our client’s schedule.
  • Please

any equipment issues to your supervisor immediately.
  • The IT team needs more time to

the cause of the system crash.
  • It took us two hours to

the issue with the payment system.
  • Constant deadlines and pressure can lead to high levels of

.
  • With three people off work, the rest of the team experienced serious

.
  • A small

between departments caused a major billing mistake.
  • The mistake in the invoice was a human

, but it affected the whole process.
  • The company decided to
the customer with a discount on their next order.

Cloze exercises are texts or sentences where students must fill in missing words. They are often used for the following purposes:

  1. To check knowledge of core course concepts (this could be a pre-check, formative exercise, or summative check).
  2. To check reading comprehension.
  3. To check vocabulary knowledge.
  4. To check word formation and/or grammatical competence.

If your goal is to test understanding of core concepts or reading comprehension

Write a summary of the concept or reading long enough to adequately test the target's knowledge, but short enough not to induce fatigue. Less than one typed page is probably adequate, but probably considerably less for young students or beginners.

Select words in the text thatare key to understanding the concepts. Thesewill probably be verbs, nouns, and key adverbs.Choose alternatives with one clear answer.

If your goal is to test vocabulary knowledge

Write a text using the target vocabulary. This text should be coherent and cohesive, and be of an appropriate length. Highlight the target words in the text. Choose alternatives with one clear answer.

If your goal is to test word formation/grammar:

Write a text using the target forms. This text should be coherent and cohesive, and be of an appropriate length. Remember that the goal is not vocabulary knowledge, so the core meanings of the stem words should be well known to the students.

Highlight the target words in the text. Provide alternatives with the same word stem, but different affixes. It is a good idea to get a colleague to test the test/exercise to make sure there are no surprises!
Cloze Activity
Enter any feedback you wish to provide the learner with-in the feedback field. This field can be left blank.
Provide instruction on how the cloze activity should be completed. Default text will be entered if there are no changes to this field.
University of Auckland
If this option is checked, submitted answers with different capitalization will be marked as incorrect.

Exercise 4 – Complete the sentences with the correct expression ✏️

Instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct expression from the list.

Expressions: make a mistake – deal with a problem – report an issue – take responsibility – follow instructions – offer compensation – prevent future errors – investigate the cause – solve the problem – share the workload

  1. The technician admitted that he had made a mistake during installation.
  2. It’s important to follow instructions carefully when using this equipment.
  3. We need to investigate the cause of the network failure before restarting the system.
  4. Please report an issue to the supervisor immediately.
  5. The company decided to offer compensation to the affected customers.
  6. Let’s meet this afternoon to deal with the problem as a team.
  7. Managers should encourage staff to share the workload during busy periods.
  8. The new checklist will help us prevent future errors .
  9. After the incident, the team leader agreed to take responsibility for the delay.
  10. I contacted the client to explain how we plan to solve the problem .
If this option is set, each word will be marked as the learner types it rather than all the words being marked the end of the exercise.

Enter the text for the cloze activity in to the cloze field by either pasting text from another source or by typing text directly into the field.

To select words to hide, double click on the word to select it and click on the Hide/Show Word button below.

More than one possible answer can be defined enclosing them with pipes (|). I.e.: |dog|cat|bird|

If left unchecked a small number of spelling and capitalization errors will be accepted. If checked only an exact match in spelling and capitalization will be accepted.

For example: If the correct answer is Elephant then both elephant and Eliphant will be judged "close enough" by the algorithm as it only has one letter wrong, even if "Check Capitalization" is on.

If capitalization checking is off in the above example, the lowercase e will not be considered a mistake and eliphant will also be accepted.

If both "Strict Marking" and "Check Capitalization" are set, the only correct answer is "Elephant". If only "Strict Marking" is checked and "Check Capitalization" is not, "elephant" will also be accepted.

Exercise 4 – Complete the sentences with the correct expression ✏️

Instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct expression from the list.

Expressions: make a mistake – deal with a problem – report an issue – take responsibility – follow instructions – offer compensation – prevent future errors – investigate the cause – solve the problem – share the workload

  1. The technician admitted that he had made a mistake during installation.
  2. It’s important to follow instructions carefully when using this equipment.
  3. We need to investigate the cause of the network failure before restarting the system.
  4. Please report an issue to the supervisor immediately.
  5. The company decided to offer compensation to the affected customers.
  6. Let’s meet this afternoon to deal with the problem as a team.
  7. Managers should encourage staff to share the workload during busy periods.
  8. The new checklist will help us prevent future errors.
  9. After the incident, the team leader agreed to take responsibility for the delay.
  10. I contacted the client to explain how we plan to solve the problem.

Exercise 4 – Complete the sentences with the correct expression ✏️

Instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct expression from the list.

Expressions: make a mistake – deal with a problem – report an issue – take responsibility – follow instructions – offer compensation – prevent future errors – investigate the cause – solve the problem – share the workload

  1. The technician admitted that he had
made a mistake

during installation.
  • It’s important to
follow instructions

carefully when using this equipment.
  • We need to
investigate the cause

of the network failure before restarting the system.
  • Please
report an issue

to the supervisor immediately.
  • The company decided to
offer compensation

to the affected customers.
  • Let’s meet this afternoon to
deal with the problem

as a team.
  • Managers should encourage staff to
share the workload

during busy periods.
  • The new checklist will help us
prevent future errors

.
  • After the incident, the team leader agreed to
take responsibility

for the delay.
  • I contacted the client to explain how we plan to
solve the problem

Cloze exercises are texts or sentences where students must fill in missing words. They are often used for the following purposes:

  1. To check knowledge of core course concepts (this could be a pre-check, formative exercise, or summative check).
  2. To check reading comprehension.
  3. To check vocabulary knowledge.
  4. To check word formation and/or grammatical competence.

If your goal is to test understanding of core concepts or reading comprehension

Write a summary of the concept or reading long enough to adequately test the target's knowledge, but short enough not to induce fatigue. Less than one typed page is probably adequate, but probably considerably less for young students or beginners.

Select words in the text thatare key to understanding the concepts. Thesewill probably be verbs, nouns, and key adverbs.Choose alternatives with one clear answer.

If your goal is to test vocabulary knowledge

Write a text using the target vocabulary. This text should be coherent and cohesive, and be of an appropriate length. Highlight the target words in the text. Choose alternatives with one clear answer.

If your goal is to test word formation/grammar:

Write a text using the target forms. This text should be coherent and cohesive, and be of an appropriate length. Remember that the goal is not vocabulary knowledge, so the core meanings of the stem words should be well known to the students.

Highlight the target words in the text. Provide alternatives with the same word stem, but different affixes. It is a good idea to get a colleague to test the test/exercise to make sure there are no surprises!
Cloze Activity
Enter any feedback you wish to provide the learner with-in the feedback field. This field can be left blank.
Juan Ignacio Plaza Rodríguez

Todas las imágenes incluidas en esta unidad han sido generadas mediante inteligencia artificial a través de la herramienta ChatGPT (OpenAI). Estas imágenes han sido creadas exclusivamente para uso educativo y no reproducen contenido preexistente. No están sujetas a derechos de autor de terceros y pueden ser utilizadas dentro del marco del proyecto educativo sin fines comerciales. Se aplican los términos de uso y licencias correspondientes al servicio de generación de imágenes de OpenAI, que permiten su uso para fines educativos y de desarrollo de materiales propios.
Unit 4: Problem solving at work - Session 1
creative commons: attribution - share alike 4.0

No est\xc3\xa1n sujetas a derechos de autor de terceros y pueden ser utilizadas dentro del marco del proyecto educativo sin fines comerciales.\nSe aplican los t\xc3\xa9rminos de uso y licencias correspondientes al servicio de generaci\xc3\xb3n de im\xc3\xa1genes de OpenAI, que permiten su uso para fines educativos y de desarrollo de materiales propios.
